'''Todd Carney''' (born 2 June 1986), also known by the [[nickname]] of "'''Toddy'''", is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] player who played in the 2000s and 2010s.

He played for the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]], [[Sydney Roosters]] (with whom he won a [[Dally M Medal]]), and the [[Canberra Raiders]] in the [[National Rugby League]] (2004 - 2014); and for [[Hull Kingston Rovers]], [[Salford Red Devils]], and the [[Catalans Dragons]] teams in the [[Super League]] (2015–2018). He debuted in the NRL from the bench, before starting at {{rlp|hb}}. He predominately played as a {{rlp|fe}} during his career, and had a brief stint as a {{rlp|fb}} in 2010.

On November 31, 2024 it was reported that Carney landed a coaching job within the NRL [[Gold Coast Titans]].<ref name="couriermail.com.au">[https://www.couriermail.com.au/subscribe/news/1/?sourceCode=CMWEB_MRE170_a_GGL&dest=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.couriermail.com.au%2Fsport%2Fnrl%2Fformer-dally-m-medal-winner-todd-carney-now-working-with-the-gold-coast-titans%2Fnews-story%2F50544f4b441e45596bf6fb7e265f48bd&memtype=anonymous&mode=premium&v21=GROUPA-Segment-1-NOSCORE]</ref>

==Background==
Carney was born in [[Goulburn]], [[New South Wales]], Australia.

==Playing career==
===Early career===
Carney played his junior [[rugby league]] for the [[Gymea Bay|Goulburn Stockmen]], before being recruited to the [[Canberra Raiders]]' junior academy at the age of just twelve. Playing in the [[S.G. Ball Cup]], Carney's team won the premiership in 2003.

===Senior career===
===Canberra Raiders (2004–2009)===
Carney made his [[National Rugby League]] Premiership début during the [[2004 NRL season]] at the age of 17. After a handful of appearances, he was named the [[Canberra Raiders]]' ''[[Rookie of the Year (award)|'Rookie of the Year]].'''<ref name="bio">{{cite web|title=Biography|work=Todd Carney Official Website|url=http://fansite.sportsnewsfirst.com.au/fansite/toddcarney/bio/|year=2011|access-date=8 September 2011|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120323055303/http://fansite.sportsnewsfirst.com.au/fansite/toddcarney/bio/|archive-date=23 March 2012}}</ref>

[[Canberra Raiders]] had signed [[Jason Smith (rugby league)|Jason Smith]] and Sam Peez, who in 2005, helped shape Carney's game.

Despite only playing 11 games and scoring a single try. Carney was rewarded with a starting position in the [[Junior Kangaroos]]' annual fixture against [[Papua New Guinea national rugby league team|Papua New Guinea]], where he tallied a record 20 points.

2006 was Carney's breakthrough year, where he cemented his position in the first-grade team, scoring 12 tries in 18 games.

Alongside [[Adam Mogg]], he was the [[Canberra Raiders|Raiders]]' leading try-scorer for 2006. His long-range kicking game also became a feature of his play, going onto record numerous 40-20's.{{citation needed|date=May 2023}}

Carney won three games in extra-time off 40-metre drop-goals, as [[Canberra Raiders|Canberra]] finished in the top 8 of the [[National Rugby League]] (NRL) competition ladder. He was again selected in the [[Junior Kangaroos]]' squad, where he was appointed as captain.<ref name="bio"/>

After an incident on 16 December 2006 in [[Goulburn]], Carney was charged with [[drink driving]] and [[reckless driving]]. Following legal proceedings in February 2007, Carney was suspended from driving a motor vehicle for five-years.<ref name="SMH-2011-02-26">{{cite news|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/im-off-the-booze-says-shaken-carney-20110226-1b95t.html|title='I'm off the booze,' says shaken Carney|author=Rakiz, Josh|date=27 February 2011|work=The Sun-Herald|location=Australia|access-date=8 September 2011 }}</ref>

On 12 June 2007, Carney appeared at the [[Magistrates Court of the Australian Capital Territory|ACT Magistrates Court]]. Carney pleaded guilty to the charges of failing to stop when directed by police, negligent driving and driving while disqualified.<ref name=smh2>{{cite news|url=http://www.leaguehq.com.au/news/news/carney-pleads-guilty/2007/06/12/1181414264172.html|title=Carney pleads guilty|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|date=12 June 2007|access-date=14 June 2007|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070927012208/http://www.leaguehq.com.au/news/news/carney-pleads-guilty/2007/06/12/1181414264172.html|archive-date=27 September 2007 }}</ref>

He was spared jail, but the Court placed Carney on a 12-month good behaviour order and banned him from driving until 2012. He was also sentenced to 200 hours of [[community service]].<ref name=smh1>{{cite news|url=http://www.leaguehq.com.au/news/raiders/banned-until-2012/2007/06/13/1181414347961.html|title=Banned until 2012|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|date=13 June 2007|access-date=13 June 2007|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070615134914/http://www.leaguehq.com.au/news/raiders/banned-until-2012/2007/06/13/1181414347961.html|archive-date=15 June 2007 }}</ref><ref name="SMH-2011-02-26"/><ref name=tdt2>{{cite news|url=http://www.news.com.au/story/0,23599,21893246-421,00.html|title=NRL star given community service|work=The Daily Telegraph|location=Australia|date=12 June 2007|access-date=14 June 2007|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090519064456/http://www.news.com.au/story/0,23599,21893246-421,00.html|archive-date=19 May 2009 }}</ref>

The Magistrate warned Carney that if he were to commit another offence he would, "undoubtedly go to jail".<ref name=smh2/>

Controversially the Canberra Raiders club sacked [[Steve Irwin (rugby league)|Steve Irwin]] (who was not charged with any offences over the incident), but retained Carney.<ref name=tdt1>{{cite news|url = http://www.news.com.au/dailytelegraph/story/0,,21823673-5006066,00.html|title = Carney spared, Irwin sacked|work = The Daily Telegraph|date = 31 May 2007|access-date =14 June 2007|first = Josh|last=Massoud}}</ref>

Carney was the Raiders' top try-scorer for the [[2007 NRL season]].<ref name="bio"/>

Before the [[2008 NRL season]], Carney began modelling his game on [[Johnathan Thurston]], in a bid to play representative football. He was also being personally coached by his life and childhood hero, [[Laurie Daley]].<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,23156172-23214,00.html|title=Carney studies JT to improve game|agency=[[Australian Associated Press]]|date=4 February 2008|access-date=8 September 2011|work=Fox Sports News|location=Australia }}</ref>

His 2008 pre-season was eventful, as he became the subject of a three-way battle between [[Canberra Raiders|Canberra]], [[Manly Sea Eagles|Manly]] and [[Penrith Panthers|Penrith]]. Eventually he re-signed with the [[Canberra Raiders|Raiders]] until 2010, with an option for a further two-years.<ref>{{cite news|title=Todd Carney re-signs with Raiders|work=[[SBS (Australian TV channel)|SBS TV]]|location=Australia|date=14 March 2008|url=http://news.sbs.com.au/worldnewsaustralia/todd_carney_resigns_with_raiders_542789 }} {{dead link|date=December 2016|bot=InternetArchiveBot|fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>

Though off-field misbehaviour continued to plague Carney's playing career and on 20 July 2008, it was "alleged" that he had urinated on a man in a [[Canberra]] nightclub, the man had to undertake counseling to overcome the trauma of the incident.<ref>{{cite news|author1 = Balym, Todd|author2 = Veness, Peter|location = Australia|work = Fox Sports News|title = Carney faces sack from NRL|url = http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,24059918-23214,00.html|access-date =22 July 2008|date = 25 July 2008 }}</ref>

Carney was suspended from participating in league matches and training while the allegations were investigated. The complaint was withdrawn two-days later, but Carney remained suspended for the round 21 match against the [[Gold Coast Titans]].<ref>{{cite news|author = Jancetic, Steve|location = Australia|work = Fox Sports News|title = Carney scratched for Titans clash|url = http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,24065296-23214,00.html|access-date =23 July 2008|date = 25 July 2008}}</ref>

The nightclub allegations sparked renewed interest in Carney's 2007 negligent driving charges, when [[Steve Irwin (rugby league)|Steve Irwin]] "claimed" that Carney was intoxicated whilst driving at the time of the 2007 incident. But [[Steve Irwin (rugby league)|Irwin]] had been pressured by the club ([[Canberra Raiders]]) to remain quiet, in an attempt to avoid a jail sentence for Carney.<ref>{{cite news|author = Ritchie, Dean|location = Australia|work = Fox Sports News|title = I'm ready to reveal names|url = http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,24075189-23214,00.html|access-date =25 July 2008|date = 25 July 2008 }}</ref>

The incident led to the club imposing an ultimatum on Carney which included a five-point plan.

Whereby Carney would have to:

*Stand-down from training and matches for the rest of the season.
*To go on an alcohol ban until the end of 2012.
*Undergo counselling.
*Complete a [[community service]] programme.
*To also pay a $20,000 fine.

The [[NRL]] threatened to de-register his contract for two-years, if Carney did not accept this plan in order to protect the [[Canberra Raiders|Raiders]] from losing him to another club.<ref>{{cite news|url = http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,24123646-23214,00.html|title = Say yes or miss next season|author = Kent, Paul|date = 4 August 2008|location = Australia|work = Fox Sports News}}</ref>

Carney asked for a variation of the plan where he could avoid points one and two.

This was unacceptable to the club's board and on 7 August 2008, the [[Canberra Raiders|Canberra]] club sacked Carney and terminated his $400,000 a season contract.{{citation needed|date=May 2023}}

Carney was de-registered by the NRL and he would not be eligible to compete in the competition until 2010.<ref>{{cite news|url = http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,24142559-23214,00.html|title = Raiders sack Carney|location = Australia|work = Fox Sports News}}</ref><ref name="Carney shocked with outcome">{{cite web|url=http://sportal.com.au/league-news-display/carney-shocked-with-outcome-54035|title=Carney shocked with outcome|location=Australia|work=Sportal|date=7 August 2008|access-date=7 August 2008|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120516004149/http://www.sportal.com.au/league-news-display/carney-shocked-with-outcome-54035|archive-date=16 May 2012 }}</ref>

Carney attempted to follow [[St. George Illawarra Dragons|ex-Dragon's]] coach [[Nathan Brown (rugby league born 1973)|Nathan Brown]] to the [[Huddersfield Giants]] on a one-year deal, but was unable to obtain a [[Working Holiday Visa|Visa]] in late 2008, due to his previous [[drink driving]] offences.<ref>{{cite news|work = BBC Sport|url = https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/huddersfield/7817444.stm|title = Huddersfield abandon Carney deal|date = 7 August 2008|access-date =9 January 2009}}</ref><ref name="Unwanted man">{{cite news|author=Fitzgerald, Peter|location=Australia|work=The Canberra Times|url=http://www.canberratimes.com.au/news/local/sport/rugby-league/unwanted-man-nrl-raiders-rule-out-giving-carney-lifeline/1403082.aspx|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090119214605/http://www.canberratimes.com.au/news/local/sport/rugby-league/unwanted-man-nrl-raiders-rule-out-giving-carney-lifeline/1403082.aspx|url-status=dead|archive-date=19 January 2009|title=Unwanted man: NRL, Raiders rule out giving Carney lifeline|date=9 January 2009|access-date=9 January 2009}}</ref>

Carney applied to the NRL in an effort to play for an Australian club in the [[2009 NRL season]], but the NRL stated that, "they would not accept registration of a contract for Todd Carney for that season".<ref name="Unwanted man"/>

In February 2009, Carney went on a vandalism spree in [[Goulburn, New South Wales|Goulburn]], jumping on a car bonnet and damaging the entrance to a [[Fone Zone]] store.{{citation needed|date=May 2023}}

He received a 12-month [[suspended jail sentence]] and he was also ordered to undertake alcohol [[counselling]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Carney run out of town|url=http://www.canberratimes.com.au/news/local/news/general/carney-run-out-of-town/1455694.aspx?storypage=0|location=Australia|work=The Canberra Times|author1=Jean, David|author2=Stockham, David|date=11 March 2009|access-date=11 March 2009|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090519222911/http://www.canberratimes.com.au/news/local/news/general/carney-run-out-of-town/1455694.aspx?storypage=0|archive-date=19 May 2009 }}</ref>

In March 2009, the [[Canberra Raiders]] agreed to release Carney to play for the [[Atherton Roosters]] in the [[Cairns]] competition in [[Far North Queensland]].<ref>{{cite news|author=Laine Clark|work = Fox Sports News (Australia)|title=Todd Carney released by Canberra Raiders to play for Atherton|url= http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,25147508-23214,00.html|access-date=6 March 2009|date=6 March 2009}}</ref>

Carney was assaulted by four-men in [[Atherton, Queensland|Atherton]] on 10 May 2009, with one of those men former [[North Queensland Cowboys|Cowboys]], [[Brisbane Broncos|Broncos]] and current (at the time of the incident) [[London Broncos]]' player, [[Nick Slyney]].<ref>{{cite news|url = http://www.smh.com.au/news/lhqnews/carney-bashed-in-atherton/2009/05/12/1241893966223.html|title = Carney bashed in Atherton|date = 12 May 2009|agency = Australian Associated Press|work = The Sydney Morning Herald|access-date =8 June 2009}}</ref>

===Sydney Roosters (2010–2011)===
After his season-long ban from the NRL expired, Carney joined the [[Sydney Roosters]] for the [[2010 NRL season]].

[[File:Todd Carney.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Carney playing for the [[Sydney Roosters]].]]
In preparation for his comeback season, Carney bought a punching bag to exercise with.<ref>{{cite news|author = Phelps, James|work = The Daily Telegraph|location = Australia|title = Todd punches on|url = http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/todd-punches-on/news-story/b628653add931d1bf9174f111c071308|date = 23 December 2009|access-date =23 December 2009}}</ref>

He played his first game for the club against his old team the [[Atherton Roosters]], in a pre-season trial in [[Atherton, Queensland|Atherton]].<ref>{{cite news|author = Greenwood, Emma|work = The Cairns Post|location = Australia|title = Carney's Sydney Roosters crush Atherton|url = http://www.cairns.com.au/article/2010/02/13/93461_local-sport-news.html|date = 13 February 2010|access-date =15 February 2010 }}</ref> After playing a lot of his football at [[Canberra Raiders|Canberra]] as a {{rlp|hb}}, he was shifted to {{rlp|fb}} by [[Sydney Roosters|Roosters]]' coach, [[Brian Smith (rugby league, born 1954)|Brian Smith]]. After his début against the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]] in a 36-10 victory, Carney was kept at {{rlp|fb}}, only shifting back into the {{rlp|hb}} position to cover for [[Mitchell Pearce]]. In round 14 against the [[Melbourne Storm]] he was permanently switched into the halves, displacing captain [[Braith Anasta]] to {{rlp|lk}}.<ref>{{cite news|url = http://www.foxsports.com.au/league/nrl-premiership/roosters-keep-todd-carney-in-halves-for-vital-warriors-clash/story-fn2mcuj6-1225921818767|title = Roosters keep Todd Carney in halves for vital Warriors clash|author = Horne, Ben|agency = Australian Associated Press|date = 22 June 2010|work = Fox Sports News|location = Australia|access-date =9 May 2011}}</ref>

His return to the halves was very successful, with the [[Sydney Roosters|Roosters]] undertaking a five-game winning streak soon after the move. Carney as {{rlp|fe}} formed an incisive attacking combination with {{rlp|hb|}} [[Mitchell Pearce]]. He was also a facilitator to his outside backs with [[Anthony Minichiello]] and [[Shaun Kenny-Dowall]] in particular showing increased productivity. In addition, he had taken on the goal kicking duties for the [[Sydney Roosters|Roosters]] scoring over 200 points.<ref>{{cite web|title = Manly Sea Eagles vs Sydney Roosters|url = http://www.nrl.com/tabid/10182/feed/videosearch/videotabid/71/contentid/292151|access-date =29 August 2010}} {{Dead link|date=May 2011}}</ref>

In the regular season Carney finished second on the top-point scorers and goal-kickers lists. He was named as the season's ''[[Dally M Awards|'Dally M Medallist']]'' for the NRL's best player.<ref>{{cite news|url = http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/todd-carney-wins-dally-m-medal/story-e6frfgbo-1225915576909|title = Todd Carney wins Dally M medal|date = 8 September 2010|work = Herald Sun|location = Australia|access-date =7 September 2010}}</ref> Carney also won the ''[[Dally M Awards|Dally M]]'' 'Five-Eight of the Year' and the ''[[Provan-Summons Medal]] ('People's Choice') Award. He played at Five-Eighth for the Roosters in their 2010 NRL Grand Final loss to the St. George IIIawarra Dragons.''

He also received the ''[[Rugby League International Federation]]'''s 'International Player of the Year Award' for 2010.<ref>{{cite web|title=Awards|url=http://www.rlif.com/awards|work=rlif.com|publisher=[[Rugby League International Federation]]|access-date=5 December 2013}}</ref>

In December 2010, it was reported that Carney had injured himself, after falling from his balcony while trying to gain entry to the building after locking himself out. He suffered no serious injuries.<ref>{{cite news|work = The Daily Telegraph|location = Australia|title = Todd Carney injured in balcony fall|url = http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/todd-carney-injured-in-balcony-fall/story-e6frexnr-1225966596103|author = Ritchie, Dean|date = 7 December 2010|access-date =9 May 2011 }}</ref>
Carney dated [[Seven Network]] television presenter, [[Liz Cantor]]. Their relationship didn't last very long and they soon went their separate ways.<ref>{{cite news|url = http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/entertainment/sydney-confidential/todd-carney-kicks-a-love-goal/story-e6frewz0-1225967211617|title = Todd Carney kicks a love goal|work = The Daily Telegraph|location = Australia|date = 8 December 2010|access-date =9 May 2011 }}</ref>
At the end of his [[2011 NRL season]], he had played a total of 118 games, 115 at club level and 3 at representative level.

On 26 February 2011, it was reported that Carney would face a low-range [[drink driving]] charge.{{citation needed|date=June 2025}} The [[Sydney Roosters]] confirmed that they would stand-by him through this indiscretion. Just over a month later, Carney was found to be involved with [[Anthony Watts (rugby league)|Anthony Watts]], when he was drinking on 16 April 2011. Following the second incident in three-months, NRL management stood Carney down from the [[Sydney Roosters]] indefinitely. He was subsequently required to receive treatment for his behavioural issues.{{citation needed|date=June 2025}}

Carney returned to the NRL in a round 10 clash against the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]] at Toyota Park.<ref>{{cite news|url = http://www.theaustralian.com.au/news/sport/sydney-roosters-stand-down-todd-carney-anthony-watts/story-e6frg7mf-1226041717291|title = Sydney Roosters stand down Todd Carney, Anthony Watts|work = The Australian|author = Read, Brent|date = 19 April 2011|access-date =9 May 2011 }}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url = http://www.foxsports.com.au/league/nrl-premiership/sydney-roosters-boss-steve-noyce-to-decide-whether-or-not-todd-carney-can-attend-anzac-day-match/story-fn2mcuj6-1226044315070|title = Sydney Roosters tell Todd Carney to stay away from Anzac Day match against St George Illawarra|work = Fox Sports News|location = Australia|author = Honeysett, Stuart|date = 25 April 2011|access-date =9 May 2011 }}</ref>
In early August 2011, Carney, together with [[Nate Myles]] and [[Frank-Paul Nuuausala]], broke a team agreement to not touch alcohol for two-weeks. All three players were subject to disciplinary hearings.<ref>{{cite news|url = http://www.theaustralian.com.au/news/sport/todd-carneys-futurs-rests-with-booze-probe/story-e6frg7mf-1226111846658|title = Todd Carney's future rests with booze probe|work = The Australian|author = Read, Brent|date = 9 August 2011|access-date =9 August 2011 }}</ref>
On 8 September 2011, it was announced that the [[Sydney Roosters]] would release Carney from the final year of his contract, following a request from Carney's management.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.theaustralian.com.au/news/sport/todd-carney-parts-company-with-the-sydney-roosters/story-e6frg7mf-1226132427741|title=Todd Carney parts company with the Sydney Roosters|author=Read, Brent|work=The Australian|date=8 September 2011|access-date=8 September 2011 }}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/todd-carney-released-by-roosters-20110908-1jziq.html|title=Todd Carney released by Roosters|agency=Australian Associated Press|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|date=8 September 2011|access-date=8 September 2011 }}</ref>

[[File:Todd_Carney_2012.jpg|thumb|left|200px|Carney at the [[2012 Dally M Awards]].]]

Denied a visa to gain access into the [[United Kingdom]] due to his criminal convictions, Carney's management were reportedly considering a contract with [[France|French-based]] [[Super League]] team, the [[Catalans Dragons]].<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2011-09-08/carney-released-by-roosters/2877248/?site=sport&section=rugbyleague|title=Carney released by Roosters|work=ABC News|location=Australia|date=8 September 2011|access-date=8 September 2011|agency=Australian Associated Press}}</ref><ref name="CM-2011-09-09">{{cite news|url=http://www.couriermail.com.au/sport/nrl/north-queensland-cowboys-coach-neil-henry-rules-out-making-a-mvoe-to-sign-todd-carney/story-e6frep5x-1226132963852|title=North Queensland Cowboys coach Neil Henry rules out making a move to sign Todd Carney|work=Courier-Mail|agency=Australian Associated Press|location=Australia|date=9 September 2011|author=McCullough, Ian|access-date=24 September 2011 }}</ref>

By mid-October 2011, there was further media speculation that Carney would be signed by the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]] or the [[St. George Illawarra Dragons]].<ref>{{cite news|author=Ritchie, Dean|url=http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/cronulla-sharks-circling-todd-carney/story-e6frexnr-1226135159977|title=Cronulla Sharks circling Todd Carney|work=The Daily Telegraph|location=Australia|date=13 September 2011|access-date=23 October 2011 }}</ref><ref>{{cite news|author=Ritchie, Dean|url=http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/cronulla-sharks-wont-rule-out-todd-carney/story-e6frexnr-1226136179765|title=Cronulla Sharks won't rule out Todd Carney|work=The Daily Telegraph|location=Australia|date=14 September 2011|access-date=23 October 2011 }}</ref><ref>{{cite news|author=Hooper, James|url=http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/st-geaorge-illawarra-cronulla-sharks-and-the-north-queensland-cowboys-in-a-tug-of-war-for-todd-carneys-signature/story-e6frfgbo-1226173934480|title=St Geaorge Illawarra, Cronulla Sharks and the North Queensland Cowboys in a tug-of-war for Todd Carney's signature|work=The Sunday Telegraph|location=Australia|date=23 October 2011|access-date=23 October 2011 }}</ref>
On 26 October 2011, it was announced that Carney had accepted a two-year contract with the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]], estimated to be worth $700,000.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/last-chance-carney-signs-twoyear-deal-with-cronulla-20111026-1mj4z.html#ixzz1c9RmzvyI|title='Last chance': Carney signs two-year deal with Cronulla|date=26 October 2011|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|access-date=30 October 2011 }}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/mother-carneys-warning-to-son-20111026-1mk8y.html|title=Mother Carney's warning to son|author=Robinson, Georgina|date=27 October 2011|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|access-date=30 October 2011 }}</ref>

===Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks (2012–2014)===
Carney's first year at [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks|Cronulla]] was very good, as he helped the side into the finals. However, they were defeated by the [[Canberra Raiders|Raiders]] in the Qualifying Finals.

He was also selected to represent [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] in the annual [[State of Origin series|State of Origin]] series playing at the {{rlp|fe}} position.

Carney spent the off-season recovering from an [[achilles tendon]] tear, but despite an offer from the [[New Zealand Warriors]] in January 2013, Carney signed-up with the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks|Sharks]] for an additional five-years from 2013.<ref>{{cite news|last=Massoud|first=Josh|title=New Zealand Warriors make shock bid for Cronulla Sharks playmaker Todd Carney|url=http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/new-zealand-warriors-make-shock-bid-for-cronulla-sharks-playmaker-todd-carney/story-e6frfgbo-1226546737697|access-date=16 February 2013|newspaper=Herald Sun|date=2 January 2013}}</ref>

Carney for the second time in his career, he won the ''[[Dally M Awards|Dally M]]'' 'Five-Eighth of the Year' Award in the [[2013 NRL season]].

On 29 June 2014, Carney was sacked by the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks|Sharks]], due to a photograph leaked on [[social media]] in which he appears to urinate into his own mouth.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Brennan|first1=Joseph|title=The Todd Carney "bubbling" social media scandal|journal=Popular Communication|volume=14|issue=4|pages=199–211|date=2016|doi=10.1080/15405702.2016.1173216|s2cid=147003423}}</ref><ref name="autogenerated1">{{cite web|title=Todd Carney reportedly sacked by Cronulla Sharks after a picture showing him urinating into his own mouth|url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2014-06-29/todd-carney-reportedly-sacked-over-social-media-photo/5557972|website=abc.net.au|access-date=29 June 2014|date=2014-06-29}}</ref>

As he was on his last warning for behavioural issues, the NRL's Chief Operating Officer Jim Doyle, said, "I would be surprised if any club would show interest in signing him. Considering his chequered history and even if they did it was 'unlikely' the NRL would agree to register him".<ref>{{cite web |title=It's all over: NRL bans Todd Carney for life |url=http://www.theaustralian.com.au/sport/nrl/its-all-over-nrl-bans-todd-carney-for-life/story-fnca0von-1226972926592 |website=www.theaustralian.com.au |publisher=[[The Australian]] |access-date=20 May 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140716011550/http://www.theaustralian.com.au/sport/nrl/its-all-over-nrl-bans-todd-carney-for-life/story-fnca0von-1226972926592 |archive-date=July 16, 2014 |language=en |date=July 1, 2014 |url-access=subscription}}</ref>

On 18 January 2019, Carney appeared on the NRL podcast titled "Bloke in a bar".  Carney was asked by host [[Denan Kemp]] about the incident which saw his contract terminated by Cronulla.  Carney said that his "Bubbler" act had been performed by another teammate [[Bryce Gibbs (rugby league)|Bryce Gibbs]].  Carney claimed the "Bubbler" became part of celebrations after each win.

Carney also claimed that former Cronulla boss Steve Noyce was among the club officials to witness Gibbs do the '"Bubbler" and says he did not balk at it before the publicly circulated photo of Carney stole headlines and ultimately led to his exile from the NRL.  Carney stated that Noyce only made an issue of the act because of the photograph becoming public.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://wwos.nine.com.au/nrl/nrl-todd-carney-bubbler-reveals-cronulla-sharks-teammate-introduced-bizarre-celebration/429c01be-6714-49c0-8fd9-f3cef5c2eee5/|title=Todd Carney claims former teammate started 'bubbler' celebration at Cronulla Sharks|website=wwos.nine.com.au}}</ref>

[[File:Todd Carney Cronulla.jpg|thumb|right|Carney with the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]] during his time at the club.]]

===Catalans Dragons (2015–2016)===
At the end of 2014, Carney moved to [[France]], after signing with [[Super League]] club the [[Catalans Dragons]], to play for them in the 2015 and 2016 [[rugby league]] seasons.<ref>{{cite news|title=Todd Carney signs for France's Catalan (sic) Dragons|url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/sport/league/10509922/Todd-Carney-signs-for-Frances-Catalan-Dragons|access-date=9 April 2015|work=stuff.co.nz|date=17 September 2014}}</ref>

In just his second game for the [[Catalans Dragons|Dragons]], Carney suffered broken ribs during a tackle by former [[Sydney Roosters|Roosters]]' teammate, [[Lama Tasi]]. The game ended in a 40-40 draw.

[[File:Todd Carney dragons.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Carney playing for [[Catalans Dragons]] in 2015.]]

===Salford Red Devils (2017)===
Carney played for the [[Salford Red Devils]] in 2017's [[Super League XXII]]. At the end of 2017, Carney signed a contract to play with [[Queensland Cup]] side, the [[Northern Pride RLFC|Northern Pride]] in a bid to revive his career in Australia.<ref>{{Cite web | url=https://wwos.nine.com.au/2017/10/27/06/48/nrl-bad-boy-todd-carney-to-play-in-the-queensland-cup-for-northern-pride | title=Former NRL bad boy Carney to return to Australia}}</ref>

===Northern Pride (2018)===
On 14 February 2018, Carney played his first game in Australia for nearly 4-years, in a trial-match for the [[Northern Pride RLFC|Northern Pride]] against the [[Mackay Cutters]].

On 2 March 2018, Carney's bid to return to the NRL suffered a setback, as [[Manly Sea Eagles]] announced they would not be signing him for the [[2018 NRL season]].<ref>{{Cite web | url=http://www.sportingnews.com/au/league/news/matt-lodge-todd-carney-nrl-double-standards-brisbane-broncos/l9tc6jgypt4q1brrdc5wuzk6p | title=NRL's double standards slammed over Matt Lodge and Todd Carney situations| date=2 March 2018}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web | url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2018/02/14/todd-carney-stars-in-northern-pride-cameo/ | title=Todd Carney stars in Northern Pride cameo| date=2018-02-13}}</ref>

===North Queensland Cowboys (2018)===
On 12 April 2018, Carney was reportedly training with the [[North Queensland Cowboys]], although not yet cleared to play by the NRL. On 2 May 2018, Carney left the [[North Queensland Cowboys|Cowboys]] to return to [[Sydney]] due to family reasons.<ref>{{Cite web | url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2018/04/11/cowboys-confident-they-can-turn-their-season-around | title=Cowboys eye turnaround in form as Todd Carney joins training| date=2018-04-11}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web | url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2018/05/02/todd-carney-walkout-no-drama-for-north-queensland-cowboys-with-young-gun-jake-clifford | title=Todd Carney walkout no drama for North Queensland Cowboys with young gun Jake Clifford ready for NRL| date=2018-05-02}}</ref>

===North Sydney Bears (2018)===
On 21 May 2018, Carney signed a deal to join [[Intrust Super Premiership]] [[New South Wales]] side, the [[North Sydney Bears]].<ref>{{Cite news | url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2018-05-21/todd-carney-officially-signed-by-north-sydney-bears/9783792 | title=Todd Carney officially signs with North Sydney Bears against Rabbitohs' wishes| newspaper=ABC News| date=2018-05-21}}</ref>

On 22 May 2018, Carney's former side the [[Northern Pride RLFC|Northern Pride]] were demanding $15,000 in compensation for relocation costs.{{citation needed|date=May 2023}}

Northern Pride Chief Executive Officer Greg Dowling, said, "He (Carney) doesn't owe us money, but we want compensation. We paid him an amount to relocate here and we want that refunded. Once they pay we'll clear him. Naturally, we were chasing a number of people and when Todd agreed, we concentrated on him thinking he would be here long-term. I've spoken to his manager and to [[Greg Florimo]], so that's where things are at now".<ref>{{Cite web | url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/report-fresh-twist-as-todd-carneys-north-sydney-bears-lifeline-hits-15000-snag/news-story/90e92478eee61724f238929b007bdbc5 | title=Todd Carney's North Sydney Bears lifeline hits snag as Northern Pride demands compensation| date=2018-05-22}}</ref>

On 1 July 2018, Carney's move to the North Sydney Bears was blocked, after Carney refused to pay the Northern Pride a $15,000 release fee.{{citation needed|date=June 2025}}

Northern Pride had demanded the North Sydney Bear to pay the release fee, but they refused to do so, hoping that a deal could be arranged between Carney's management team and the [[North Queensland Cowboys]] side before the transfer window closed.<ref>{{Cite web | url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/carney-s-bears-stint-sunken-by-star-s-refusal-to-pay-15-000-fee-20180630-p4zoqo.html | title=Carney's Bears stint sunken by star's refusal to pay $15,000 fee| date=2018-06-30}}</ref>

===Hull Kingston Rovers (2018)===
It was revealed on 7 July 2018, that Carney had signed a short-term contract at [[Hull Kingston Rovers]], to play for the east [[Kingston upon Hull|Hull]] outfit until at least the end of the 2018 rugby league season.<ref>{{cite tweet|number=1015460400626724864|user=hullkrofficial|title=BREAKING: We've signed talented half back and former Australian international Todd Carney on a short-term deal unti…|date=7 July 2018}}</ref>

Just 24 hours after landing in Australia, Carney participated in his first training session with his new club on 23 July 2018.
 
On 27 July 2018, Carney made his Hull Kingston Rovers' début in the '[[Rugby league derbies in the United Kingdom|Hull Derby]]' against cross-city rivals, [[Hull F.C.]]
 
On a rain-swept evening at the [[KCOM Stadium]], Todd's contribution to the game was a positive one, coming off the interchange bench and kicking a 40-20.
 
Carney made a short cameo appearance on his Hull Kingston Rovers bow, considering he had only just arrived at his new club just five-days previous.
 
Head Coach [[Tim Sheens]] took the decision not to throw Carney into the deep end on his début, (due to his lack of game-time over the previous months), to allow him time to adjust and to get himself well adapt to his new surroundings.
 
His efforts in his first match were held in good stead, as Hull Kingston Rovers claimed a thrilling 16-20 victory in the final game of the regular [[Super League]] season.

===Warrington Wolves (2020)===

On 26 January, Carney agreed to join Warrington Wolves on a one-year deal.{{citation needed|date=June 2025}}

==Coaching/post playing career==
On November 31, 2024 it was reported that Todd Carney landed a coaching job within the NRL Gold Coast Titans.<ref name="couriermail.com.au"/>
